provenance.review_method: Model-assisted structural review of every supplied evidence note; no systematic full-text verification of the cited corpus

evidence.title: No infected source animal was ever found at the Huanan market or in its supply chain
evidence.claim: China CDC sampling of 457 animal specimens spanning ~18 species at the Huanan Seafood Market detected SARS-CoV-2 in environmental swabs but in zero animal samples, and subsequent tracing of wildlife farms and supply chains has not identified any infected progenitor or intermediate host animal. Lab-leak proponents cite this persistent absence as a notable evidentiary gap relative to SARS-CoV-1 (civets) and MERS (camels), where infected intermediate hosts were found relatively quickly.
evidence.evidence_quality:
The core factual claim — no animal sample tested positive, and no infected source animal has been identified to date — rests on **peer-reviewed primary sources** (Liu et al. in *Nature*; Crits-Christoph et al. in *Cell*; Débarre in *Virus Evolution*) plus the official WHO–China report. These are independent and mutually consistent on the factual point. The claim is well established and largely uncontested.

What is contested is the **interpretation**: whether the absence is informative about origins. This is fundamentally an absence-of-evidence argument, and the evidence base has a strong confounder — the animals were removed and the market disinfected before sampling, so a clean test was never possible.
evidence.strongest_counterargument: The natural-origin rebuttal is that the absence is fully expected and uninformative, not a genuine gap. (1) Susceptible species (raccoon dogs, civets) were genetically confirmed present in the exact stalls with the highest viral positivity (Crits-Christoph et al., 2024), so the spatial signal points where a spillover would. (2) Sampling occurred **after** the live animals had been culled/removed and surfaces cleaned, and weeks into sustained human transmission, destroying the window to catch an infected animal (Débarre, 2024). (3) For SARS-CoV-1 it took months and for MERS it took ~1–2 years to find the intermediate host even with the live trade intact and accessible — here the relevant animals and farms were depopulated under COVID controls before independent investigators had access. On this view, the negative result excludes essentially nothing, so it cannot weigh against zoonosis. (Conversely, lab-leak proponents counter that authorities had every incentive and capacity to find an infected animal and did not, and that the rapid culling itself is suspicious — but this is a circumstantial argument, not positive evidence.)
evidence.confidence_assessment:
- **Claim confidence:** 0.92 — The factual core (zero positive animal samples; no infected source animal identified to date) is solidly documented in multiple peer-reviewed primary sources. The small residual uncertainty reflects ongoing methodological disputes over the China CDC species assignments and the possibility of unreported sampling.
- **Support strength:** weak — For lab-leak. The absence is real, but it is heavily confounded by pre-sampling depopulation and disinfection, so it is close to non-diagnostic between hypotheses. It removes one piece of confirmatory evidence a zoonosis would ideally leave, without supplying any positive evidence of a lab origin.
- **Net:** A calibrated reasoner should treat "no positive source animal" as a true but only mildly informative fact: it modestly lowers confidence in a *fully confirmed* zoonotic chain, but because susceptible animals were demonstrably present and then removed before testing, it does not meaningfully raise the probability of a laboratory origin.

citations.citation: Liu, W.J., Liu, P., Lei, W., et al. (2023). Surveillance of SARS-CoV-2 at the Huanan Seafood Market. *Nature*, 612–620. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-023-06043-2

Crits-Christoph, A., Pekar, J.E., Débarre, F., et al. (2024). Genetic tracing of market wildlife and viruses at the epicenter of the COVID-19 pandemic. *Cell*, 187(19), 5468–5482.e11. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cell.2024.08.010 (PubMed: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37745602/)
Débarre, F. (2024). What we can and cannot learn from SARS-CoV-2 and animals in metagenomic samples from the Huanan market. *Virus Evolution*, 10(1), vead077. https://doi.org/10.1093/ve/vead077

WHO (2021). WHO-convened global study of origins of SARS-CoV-2: China Part (joint WHO–China report). https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/who-convened-global-study-of-origins-of-sars-cov-2-china-part

Science Media Centre (2023). Expert reaction to the Liu et al. environmental-sampling study (sample counts and critiques, incl. Prof. Alice Hughes on species-assignment concerns). https://www.sciencemediacentre.org/expert-reaction-to-study-looking-at-sars-cov-2-in-environmental-samples-from-the-huanan-seafood-market/

framing_assumptions: A zoonotic market origin should have produced a detectable infected animal despite the delay and removal of live animals.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on the counterfactual probability of detection given actual sampling timing and supply-chain disruption.
coverage_gaps: No contemporaneous animal testing occurred at the market, and upstream farms and suppliers were incompletely investigated.
correlation_risks: Narrower version of the no-close-progenitor/intermediate-host evidence and correlated with market-animal items.
capability_limits: Assessing sampling, ascertainment, and spatial or temporal inference requires raw data and specialist statistical review beyond the supplied summary.

evidential: No positive source animal has been reported; the expected detection rate is unresolved.
ingestion_conditional: Late and incomplete sampling makes the negative result coverage-dependent.
framing_conditional: Not finding an animal is not equivalent to sampling the relevant animals and obtaining negative results.

notes: Represent as a failed-detection claim with explicit search coverage.
This is a substantive structural annotation of the supplied evidence note, not an independent finding that its factual claim is true.
